Concerning the nature of Deity: that it is incomprehensible.

metadata.author: John of Damascus: Exposition of the Orthodox Faith

ccel.title: NPNF2-09. Hilary of Poitiers, John of Damascus

source.title: NPNF2-09. Hilary of Poitiers, John of Damascus

title: Concerning the nature of Deity: that it is incomprehensible.

source.title: NPNF2-09. Hilary of Poitiers, John of Damascus

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: It is plain, then, that there is a God.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: But what He is in His essence and nature is absolutely incomprehensible and unknowable.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For it is evident that He is incorporeal.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For how could that possess body which is infinite, and boundless, and formless, and intangible and invisible, in short, simple and not compound?

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: How could that be immutable which is circumscribed and subject to passion?

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: And how could that be passionless which is composed of elements and is resolved again into them?

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For combination is the beginning of conflict, and conflict of separation, and separation of dissolution, and dissolution is altogether foreign to God.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: Again, how will it also be maintained that God permeates and fills the universe? as the Scriptures say, Do not I fill heaven and earth, saith the Lord?

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For it is an impossibility that one body should permeate other bodies without dividing and being divided, and without being enveloped and contrasted, in the same way as all fluids mix and commingle.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: But if some say that the body is immaterial, in the same way as the fifth body of which the Greek philosophers speak (which body is an impossibility), it will be wholly subject to motion like the heaven.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For that is what they mean by the fifth body.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: Who then is it that moves it?

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For everything that is moved is moved by another thing.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: And who again is it that moves that? and so on to infinity till we at length arrive at something motionless.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For the first mover is motionless, and that is the Deity.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: And must not that which is moved be circumscribed in space?

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: The Deity, then, alone is motionless, moving the universe by immobility.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: So then it must be assumed that the Deity is incorporeal.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: But even this gives no true idea of His essence, to say that He is unbegotten, and without beginning, changeless and imperishable, and possessed of such other qualities as we are wont to ascribe to God and His environment.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For these do not indicate what He is, but what He is not.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: But when we would explain what the essence of anything is, we must not speak only negatively.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: In the case of God, however, it is impossible to explain what He is in His essence, and it befits us the rather to hold discourse about His absolute separation from all things.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For He does not belong to the class of existing things: not that He has no existence, but that He is above all existing things, nay even above existence itself.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For if all forms of knowledge have to do with what exists, assuredly that which is above knowledge must certainly be also above essence: and, conversely, that which is above essence will also be above knowledge.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: God then is infinite and incomprehensible and all that is comprehensible about Him is His infinity and incomprehensibility.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: But all that we can affirm concerning God does not shew forth God’s nature, but only the qualities of His nature.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For when you speak of Him as good, and just, and wise, and so forth, you do not tell God’s nature but only the qualities of His nature.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: Further there are some affirmations which we make concerning God which have the force of absolute negation: for example, when we use the term darkness, in reference to God, we do not mean darkness itself, but that He is not light but above light: and when we speak of Him as light, we mean that He is not darkness.
